title: Evaluating at scale
concept: Combine automated benchmark evals (MMLU) with LLM-as-a-judge preference loops and user feedback tracking to keep models aligned in production.

exercises.prompt: How do developers inspect millions of AI answers daily without losing their minds?

options.text: Hire thousands of people to read every single chat.
Use automated benchmarks and LLM judges to grade outputs.
Turn off the AI every hour to check it manually.

exercises.explain: Evaluating at scale requires automation because humans cannot keep up with millions of interactions per day.

cards.title: Scaling Up is Hard
cards.body: When building an AI model, you can easily read 10 of its answers to see if they look good. But what happens when your AI generates 10 million answers a day? You can't read them all! Let's learn how we evaluate AI at scale to keep it friendly and safe.

cards.title: Evaluating at Scale
cards.body: Evaluating at scale means using automated tools to continuously test our AI. Instead of reading every response ourselves, we combine standardized tests (benchmarks), other AI agents as graders (LLM-as-a-judge), and user thumbs-up/down buttons to keep the AI on track.

cards.title: The Restaurant Inspector
cards.body: Think of evaluating an LLM like running a restaurant. Benchmarks are the food inspectors checking safety rules (MMLU). LLM-as-a-judge is a hired head chef tasting test plates for presentation. User feedback tracking is the comment cards left by real diners. You need all three to keep the restaurant successful!

cards.title: AI Support in Action
cards.body:
Imagine an AI customer service agent. 
1. Benchmarks (like MMLU) prove it knows basic math and policy rules.
2. An 'LLM-as-a-judge' looks at 1,000 chat logs and grades them on politeness.
3. Users click 'Thumbs Down' if the bot fails to help, highlighting immediate bugs to fix.

cards.title: The Perfect Test Score Myth
cards.body: Many believe that if an AI gets a high score on a standard benchmark test (like MMLU), it is 100% ready for real users. In reality, a model can ace a multiple-choice test but still behave rudely or give bad advice in a live chat. We need active feedback loops!

cards.title: Benchmarks vs. Live Feedback
cards.body: Using only one type of evaluation leaves blind spots. We must combine automated checks with real human signals.

left.text: Tests general knowledge and facts (like a school exam).

right.text: Reveals real-world bugs, tone issues, and actual helpfulness.

exercises.prompt: Why aren't academic benchmarks (like MMLU) enough on their own to evaluate a live chatbot?

options.text: They are too expensive to run.
They test academic knowledge, not real-world chat behavior and tone.
They can only be graded by humans.
They are written in a secret language.

exercises.explain: Academic benchmarks like MMLU are great, but they don't capture real-world user preferences, tone, or dynamic conversations in production.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once an AI passes its initial benchmarks, we should ignore thumbs-down feedback from real users.
exercises.explain: User feedback (like thumbs-up or down buttons) is incredibly valuable for finding edge cases and real-world bugs that automated tests missed.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of this automated evaluation technique.
exercises.sentence: Using one AI model to grade the output of another AI model is called LLM-as-a-_____.
exercises.explain: An 'LLM-as-a-judge' is another AI model programmed to score or evaluate the quality of responses generated by your main AI model.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a complete, scaled evaluation system for a production AI?

options.text: A system that relies solely on human engineers reading every chat log.
A system combining academic benchmarks, an LLM judge, and user thumbs-up data.
A system that only tests the AI once before it is released.

exercises.explain: Standard benchmarks test general knowledge; LLM judges grade style and safety; real user feedback (like thumbs down) flags actual production failures. All three form a complete evaluation loop.

exercises.prompt: Your new AI chat assistant is going live to millions of users today! What is the best strategy to ensure the model stays safe, accurate, and helpful at this massive scale?

options.text: Rely only on basic academic benchmarks since they cover all real-world human interactions.
Wait for users to complain on social media before testing any model updates.
Combine automated benchmarks, LLM judges for style, and real user feedback to monitor quality.
Manually read every single conversation the model has in real-time.

exercises.explain: To safely manage a model in production, we need multiple layers: standard benchmarks to check basic facts, LLMs-as-a-judge to grade open-ended conversations, and user feedback tracking to catch real-world issues.
